metric_name: ObjectBroker 2.6 release and CORBA 2.0 compliance — actual outcome
metric_value: ObjectBroker 2.6 released 1996 with CORBA 2.0 features as predicted; however DEC acquired by Compaq 1998, ObjectBroker product line discontinued; renamed Compaq ObjectBroker briefly then terminated
notes: Short-term prediction (OBS-025, OBS-028) confirmed: 2.6 shipped in 1996. Long-term: product killed by acquisition. DEC/Compaq ObjectBroker history documented.

metric_value: Mixed: CORBA adoption peaked ~1998-2002 in enterprise (telecom, finance) but web services (SOAP 1998, REST 2000) emerged as dominant integration paradigm; CORBA largely replaced by SOA/REST/microservices by 2010
notes: Prediction (OBS-047) partially correct: ORBs did grow critical in 1990s. But HTTP/SOAP/REST superseded CORBA for most enterprise integration. CORBA survived only in niche sectors (telecom CORBA via JAIN).

metric_name: ObjectBroker IBM MVS mainframe support — actual outcome
metric_value: ObjectBroker 2.6 did add MVS support as planned; however IBM's own CICS CORBA gateway and SOM/DSOM became primary IBM mainframe CORBA solutions by 1997
notes: Prediction (OBS-032) confirmed in product: MVS support shipped. But IBM offered competing mainframe CORBA solutions; DEC/Compaq acquisition in 1998 ended ObjectBroker MVS track.

metric_value: Failed: Microsoft pivoted to DCOM/COM+ and rejected CORBA interop; OMG OLE-CORBA bridging standard was published (1999) but never widely implemented; .NET (2002) replaced COM for new development
notes: Prediction (OBS-024, OBS-038) wrong: Microsoft-CORBA interop was abandoned. Microsoft chose DCOM over CORBA bridging. .NET/WCF became Windows integration standard. Aberdeen overestimated Microsoft-OMG collaboration.

metric_name: DEC ObjectBroker multi-platform ORB leadership — actual outcome
metric_value: Failed: DEC acquired by Compaq 1998; ObjectBroker discontinued; IONA Technologies Orbix, Borland VisiBroker, and then IBM WebSphere MQ became enterprise middleware leaders
notes: Prediction (OBS-036) wrong for long-term: DEC/ObjectBroker never established market leadership. IONA, Borland, and IBM dominated the ORB/middleware market that emerged.
